narrative:
President Donald Trump has launched a series of public criticisms against British Prime Minister Keir Starmer over the UK's initial refusal to allow US forces to use British military bases for strikes against Iran, repeatedly stating "This is not Winston Churchill that we're dealing with" [1][2][3].

## Initial Dispute and Resolution

The conflict centers on Operation Epic Fury, where Starmer initially blocked the US from using British military bases, specifically Diego Garcia in the Chagos Islands, for strikes against Iran [1]. The UK later permitted the use of the bases for "defensive strikes" after Trump's complaints [1]. By Sunday, the UK agreed to allow the US to use British military bases - likely RAF Fairford in Gloucestershire and Diego Garcia - but only for defensive action following Iran's retaliatory strikes [2][3].

## Trump's Public Criticism

Speaking in the Oval Office on Tuesday ahead of a meeting with German Chancellor Friedrich Merz, Trump expressed his displeasure: "By the way, I'm not happy with the U.K. either" [1]. He criticized the delay, saying "it has taken three, four days for us to work out where we can land there" and that "It would have been much more convenient landing there as opposed to flying many extra hours" [1][2][3].

This marked the third time in 24 hours that Trump had criticized Starmer [3]. He told The Sun on Monday that the "relationship is obviously not what it was" and described Starmer as having "not been helpful" [2][3].

## Starmer's Defense

Starmer has defended his position, telling Parliament on Monday: "President Trump has expressed his disagreement with our decision not to get involved in the initial strikes, but it is my duty to judge what is in Britain's national interest. That is what I've done, and I stand by it" [1][3]. He stated that the UK "does not believe in regime change from the skies" [2][3] and was "not involved in the initial strikes against Iran, and we will not join offensive action now" [1].

Starmer explained that the situation changed on Sunday when Iran's "outrageous" response became "a threat to our people, our interests and our allies," prompting the decision to allow bases to be used for defensive strikes on Tehran's missile infrastructure [2].

## Broader Diplomatic Impact

The dispute extends beyond the UK, with Trump also threatening to cut off all trade with Spain after Madrid prohibited the US from using bases on its territory for attacks on Iran [3]. European countries have reportedly struggled to find a united position on the rapidly unfolding Middle East events [3].

Former British ambassador to the US Lord Darroch described Trump's comments as "pretty brutal," noting there was "obviously a serious rift there now" and that "Trump is very unhappy about that denial of British airbases and I think that anger will hang around for a while" [2].

citations.title: Trump slams Starmer as 'not Winston Churchill' for refusal to back Iran strikes

citations.title: Trump rebukes Starmer again for not letting US attack Iran from UK bases
citations.quote: It was the third time in 24 hours that Trump had criticised Starmer for the UK's refusal to aid the initial strikes

keyDevelopments: Trump publicly criticized Starmer three times in 24 hours over UK's initial refusal to allow use of British bases for Iran strikes
UK initially blocked use of Diego Garcia and other bases for offensive strikes but later agreed to defensive use
Starmer defended decision as being in Britain's national interest and stated UK doesn't believe in 'regime change from the skies'
Trump also threatened trade consequences against Spain for similar base access refusal
Former UK ambassador characterized Trump's language as 'pretty brutal' indicating serious diplomatic rift
